title: Evening Wrap: ASX 200 slumps on panic selling in gold and silver stocks, NEM -10%, NST -8%
content:
The S&P/ASX 200 closed 90.5 points lower, down -1.02%.

The ASX 200 drifted toward a one-month low as whipsaw price action in precious metals and base metals triggered forced selling in Resources (XJR) (-3.1%) and turbocharged intraday volatility. Markets were also pricing tomorrowтАЩs first RBA meeting of 2026, with indications of a 72% chance of hike in the cash rate to 3.85%.

Contrasting weakness in miners, banks held up well, with the Financials (XFJ) (+0.1%) sector notched its second gain in two losing sessions for the benchmark. Commonwealth Bank (CBA) (+2.3%) closed off its highs but still logged a healthy gain.

Meanwhile, Information Technology (XIJ) (-1.1%) extended a five-session slide to its lowest level since February 2024.

BHP (BHP) (-2.2%) and Rio Tinto (RIO) (-1.0%) held up reasonably well among the major miners, but South32 (S32) (-4.3%) and Sandfire Resources (SFR) (-3.9%) weren't as lucky.

In stock-specific news, GrainCorp (GNC) (-14%) slid sharply after it cut its EBITDA guidance to $200тАУ240 million, well short of consensus estimates.

Be sure to click/scroll through for the usual reporting of the major sector and stock-specific moves, the broker responses to them, as well as all the key economic data in tonight's Evening Wrap.

Also, I have detailed technical analysis on the Nasdaq Composite and the S&P/ASX 200 in today's ChartWatch.

Let's dive in!

ASX 200 Session Chart

The S&P/ASX 200 (XJO) finished 90.5 points lower at 8,778.6, 1.0% from its session high and just 0.37% from its low. In the broader-based S&P/ASX 300 (XKO) decliners outnumbers advancers by a disturbing 187 to 94.

Fund flows: Investors thrown into the Warshing machine!

Trump, Warsh, gold and silver melt down...

Any number of excuses can be applied to todayтАЩs dumping of precious metals and resources stocks. Ultimately, as usual, it comes down to fund flows.

The flow of capital into those assets over the last 6-months was extreme тАФ to say is has been тАЬall demand and no supplyтАЭ during that period would only be a modest exaggeration!

Those fund flows were being driven by certain narratives of which those of us who are 100% technical analysis + trend following would care little about:

Falling real yields

De-dollarisation / divestment of US risk-free assets

Central-bank buying

Persistent geopolitical unease

Fears about US Fed independence

etc. etc!

It was the same narrative-believing buyers buying from the same reluctant narrative-believing sellers. (With a bunch of speculative narrative-believing traffic over the top! ЁЯЪА)

Then you get a bit of news, i.e., new information, that halts demand and forces a bit of supply. Hot money demand dries up, weak hands get margin calls, and then get liquidated. Positional demand stopped buying about 20% agoтАж waiting for the pullbackтАж so they're not supporting the first 20% dump... which means you're left with a demand-side vacuum.

To say the last two trading sessions have been тАЬall supply and no demandтАЭ would only be a modest exaggeration!

Where does this leave our up-until-ThursdayтАЩs-all-time-high Resources (XJR) (-3.1%) sector? On alert for me... Bruised but not broken... Yet! (see chart below)

ASX 200 Resources Sector Index (XJR) chart

You can see some demand creeping in at the short term uptrend ribbon, but also a couple of decent warning signals in Friday's and Monday's candles. Pullbacks within strong trends are very normal, you can see several since the XJRтАЩs present long term uptrend began back in September.

However, whatтАЩs not normal, is the pullback in precious metals тАФ which have been critical drivers of the XJRтАЩs rally.

Silver Futures (Front month, back-adjusted) COMEX chart

тАЬThat candleтАЭ on silver is nothing like normal тАФ it's a generational outlier. In my experience, such candles signal the end of the cycle FMOTN (Far More Often Than Not! ЁЯШЙ). This is how the silver and gold commodity price moves have spilled into the ASX тАФ via the Gold Sub-index (XGD) (-7.2%):

All Ordinaries Gold Sub-index (XGD) chart

But the XJR is far more than just a few silver and gold stocks. Look at BHP Group (BHP) (-2.3%) and Rio Tinto (RIO) (-1.0%) charts below. Arguably far less damage. So, I suggest that silverтАЩs FMOTN death-knell is far from a corresponding death sentence for the XJR.

BHP Group (BHP) chart (TOP), Rio Tinto (RIO) chart (bottom)

If you watch my ChartWatch *LIVE* Webinars, youтАЩll be familiar with my views on incremental risk management тАФ i.e., adjusting risk little by little as the demand-supply dynamics change for or against a position.

IтАЩll leave you with a question for now, but I'll aim to answer it with my charts in Wednesday's webinar: What do the charts above say about how one should manage their risks? +R, =R, or -R!? ЁЯдФ

ChartWatch
Nasdaq Composite Index
Analysis

You know all that supply weтАЩve been tracking / fretting in the 23722-24020 zone?

It looks like it got a tad more motivated on FridayтАж and that motivation was compounded with a little more size = first above average volume day in the last 6 candles.

The buy the dip demand didnтАЩt completely dissolve: above average volume represents sellers and the buyers that took on their risk. But the demand-sideтАЩs lack of motivation to compete on price hardly inspires confidence.

SilverтАЩs move on Friday is a perfect example of one of really, only a couple of chinks in the trend followerтАЩs armour: the inability to see what one cannot see тАФ i.e., an impending massive trend reversal тАФ when there are few signals in the price action and candles that one is coming.

Basically, the biggest flaw in trend following is oneтАЩs inability to tell the future! ЁЯФо

No doubt, though, there are many out there spouting useless idioms like тАЬBlind Freddy could have seen that fall comingтАЭ.

I donтАЩt know about Blind Freddy, but no doubt even professional idiom spouters have perfect 20-20 hindsight! ЁЯдУ

If youтАЩre wondering why I havenтАЩt been running regular commentary on silver and other commodities, itтАЩs because ChartWatch Markets is on hiatus. IтАЩm working on replacing it with a series of short videos that one can watch instead of having to read. Stay tuned for those in the near future. ЁЯУ║

As for silver, I did manage to provide some insights into the potential for a major reversal in Wednesday's ChartWatch *LIVE* Webinar. Watch the recording from roughly 7-10 minutes. I couldnтАЩt тАЬcall itтАЭ because I didnтАЩt have enough information in the candles.

Then, there was that candle тАФ the 30-Jan wipeout тмЫЁЯХпя╕П! Which brings me to the other chink: A one-candle move thatтАЩs so big, thereтАЩs just no time to manage oneтАЩs risk to any great magnitude in advance.

No model is infallible. Mine certainly isnтАЩt. But I take solace in the fact that neither are those of our favourite idiom spouters!

Anyways, whatтАЩs the relevance of all of this with respect to the Comp chart? ЁЯдФ

ItтАЩs this:

The technicals we can see are still demand-side control biased.

There are some credible signals in the last few candles that the supply-sideтАЩs influence is growing (bullet dodged with ThursdayтАЩs long downward pointing shadow and grazed on Friday).

If todayтАЩs ASX 200 move is a precursor to shifting global risk appetite, we may see a big candle tonight that doesnтАЩt allow a pre-emptive strike on managing risk.

Given the above, and given that unlike idiom spouters, I refuse to make predictions, I must stay the course on the Comp.

On the last point, clearly though тАФ at a heightened level of alertness тАФ preferring not to add any new long-side risk for the time-being.

I argue, however, that there isnтАЩt enough evidence in the chart above that the supply-side actually has control of the CompтАЩs price to require a move away from my present portfolio risk limit.

But consider this: the average balanced fund typically holds between 5-10% cash. Index funds are 0% cash by definition. So, my present portfolio risk limit of 2/3RP implies an already super-conservative-by-regular-industry-standards one-third cash allocation.

That's even more reason to sit on the couch with my old mate Blind Freddy and a box of popcorn tonight and watch how the candles play out! ЁЯН┐

View

I am on hold for the addition of new long-side portfolio risk, but officially, remain at my 2/3RP portfolio risk limit ЁЯкг (RP = Risk Position тАФ it reflects my personal allowable capital allocation limit for my investments in US stocks. So 1/2RP is 50%, 2/3RP is 67% and FRP is 100%). A close below 22692 would trigger a move to 1/2RP.

Key levels

The next zone of demand is 22692-916. If the Comp closes below that zone, we can assume the supply side is in control. On a close below 21898 the long term uptrend is likely under significant pressure = тЪая╕П. 23722-24020 is the nearest critical zone of supply тАФ the Comp must close within/above this zone with a strong demand-side candle to confirm the demand-side is unequivocally back in control of the Comp's price.

S&P/ASX 200 (XJO)
Analysis

Okay Fred my old mate, what do we think about this chart? ЁЯЩИ

Black candle + Decent length + Close near the low of the session = Supply-side control + highly motivated supply + motivated excess supply steady through day, little impact of bargain huntersтАж likely therefore unrequited supply remaining in the system!

Volume? (As with Friday's supply-side candle) Above average showing = decent whack of supply finding its way into the system = тЪая╕П

ThereтАЩs a few other bitsтАж like the complete uselessness of the short term uptrend ribbon тАФ thatтАЩs MOTN (More Often Than Not) associated with a market devoid of buy-the-dippersтАж plus a rather forceful tickle of the 8765 point of demand = ditto on buy-the-dippers are on strike! тЪая╕П

Put it all together and you get a giant pile of steamingтАж

HO-HUM.

You thought I was going to say something else there, didnтАЩt you! I have way too much decorum for that! ЁЯШЙ

Okay, portfolio risk management call time. IтАЩm at 2/3RP. As noted above, thatтАЩs a substantial whack of cash compared to industry standards already. But do I take an extra precautionary step and go to 1/2RP? ЁЯдФ

If youтАЩre following along at home, you know that I (you?) have been that way-or-lower-risk-rated since mid-October (i.e., 1/2RP and as low as 1/3RP).

Think about how dichotomous our market has been over that timeтАж particularly the underperformance of many of the bluest-of-blue-chip stocks in Financials, Consumer Discretionary, Technology, and Healthcare.

Do you feel like youтАЩve missed a great deal by being less than fully invested? ЁЯд╖

Consider that cash doesnтАЩt earn zero тАФ it earns a few percent (and a bit more if youтАЩre savvy). And if the market tanks from hereтАж then that cash effectively grows in purchasing power by however much the market falls. 10%? 20%? 57% like in the GFC? ЁЯдп

If the HO-HUM hits the fanтАж I suggest that my model is going to beat most passive and discretionary funds (save for those focussed on going short!).

So: 2/3RP or 1/2RP?

ItтАЩs a very close given the short term trend ribbon and 8765 have gone. LetтАЩs go with: I will suspend adding all new long-side risk exposure and let my portfolio risk reduce by тАЬnatural attritionтАЭ to 1/2RP.

A close below the long term uptrend ribbon (presently 8602-8707) would see me move to my maximum risk-averse setting of 1/3RP. LetтАЩs also be optimists, hey, why the ruddy hell not!? A close above the newly formed point of supply at 8979 would see me move back to 2/3RP again.

Oh, Old Tin PotтАж youтАЩve done it again! ЁЯШн

View

I will suspend adding all new long-side risk exposure and let my portfolio risk reduce by тАЬnatural attritionтАЭ to 1/2RP ЁЯкг.

Key levels

The long term trend ribbon is the closest zone of (dynamic) demand (presently 8602-8707). 8675 remains the closest static point of demand. A close below the long term uptrend ribbon would neutralise the present long term uptrend. 8979 is the next key zone of supply.

summary:
Here's a summary of the ASX 200 market wrap:

*   The S&P/ASX 200 experienced a significant slump, closing down 90.5 points or -1.02%, reaching a one-month low, primarily driven by panic selling in gold and silver stocks.
*   The Resources sector was heavily impacted, falling 3.1%, with notable drops in Newmont Corp. (NEM) by -10.0% and Northern Star Resources (NST) by -8.1%, while BHP and Rio Tinto held up relatively better.
*   In contrast, the Financials sector showed resilience, edging up 0.14%, with Commonwealth Bank (CBA) logging a healthy gain of +2.3%, and the Information Technology sector extended its slide, down 1.13%.
*   GrainCorp (GNC) was a significant individual mover, sliding 14% after cutting its EBITDA guidance, while upcoming RBA meeting data, with a 72% chance of a rate hike, also influenced market sentiment.
